content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
August 11, 2011 
Joan Robbins 
4524 A Parkway 
Sacramento, California 95823 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our file No. 1-11-107 
Dear Ms. Robbins: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair 
Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, 
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 Please note that our advice is based 
solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of 
other post-governmental employment laws such as Public Contract Code Section 10411. 
FACTS 
You are currently employed with the Department of Alcohol and Drug Programs 
("DADP") as a Staff Services Manager I. You were previously in a Staff Services Manager II 
position at the same agency more than a year ago. Both positions are designated in the agency's 
conflict-of-interest code. You are interested in separating from State service to work in the 
private sector. The company you are interested in working for is a provider of medical, mental 
health, and substance abuse services and owns substance abuse programs licensed by various 
State departments including the DADP. The DADP licenses and certifies this company to 
provide substance abuse services. 
As a Staff Services Manager I, you supervised staff that collected data and conducted 
research in counties within California related to the use of alcohol and drug abuse programs. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 9 IO 14. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 831 14, Regulation l 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) 


File No. I-11-107 
Page No. 2 
After collecting data, your staff analyzed and identified trends in the data. This information was 
shared with the University of California, Los Angeles, the County Alcohol and Drug Programs 
Administrators, provider organizations and other entities. The DADP would then issue an annual 
report based upon the data collected and analyzed jointly by these entities. 
Your proposed duties for the private sector employer would be: 
1. Coordinate with local agencies and government officials for the expansion and growth 
of substance abuse, mental health, and primary care treatment programs in unserved and 
underserved communities within California and other states. 
2. Attend various meetings as required to keep apprised of current and future State, 
federal and local governments' proposals, policies, laws and regulations. 
3. Assist in data collection to conduct needs assessment and outcome reporting. 
In this new position you would not have the authority to make final decisions regarding 
contracts, applications or proposals for fiscal support and/or new programs. In this new position, 
you would not be appearing before your former agency for the purpose of influencing decisions 
and your name would not be included on any correspondence between your new employer and 
your former agency. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the "one-year ban" and the "permanent ban," colloquially 
known as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 3 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state agency who holds a position that is 
designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section 87406(d) 
(1); Regulation 18746.1 (a) (2).) Because the last position you held was designated in your 
agency's conflict-of-interest code, the one-year ban applies to you. Therefore, you would be 
prohibited for one year from conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or 
by electronic communication, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication 
to your former agency if any of these actions are done for the purpose of influencing 
governmental decisions made by your former agency. (Regulation 18746.2.) The one-year ban 
does not apply to formal participation in a panel or conference for educational purposes or to 
disseminate research to your former agency, attendance at general informational meetings of 
your former agency, seminars, or similar eventsi making requests for information about'any 
matter of public record from your former agency or communications with the press. 
3 In addition, Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from making, participating in making, 
or using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, 
or have any arrangement concerning employment. (Also see Regulation 18747.) 


File No. 1-11-107 
Page No. 3 
The enclosed fact sheet "Leaving Your State Job: Revolving Door and Other Post­
Employment Issues for State Officials" describes in further detail the activities that are 
prohibited by this ban. 
The permanent ban applies to every "state administrative official," which is defined as 
"every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of 
his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in 
other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity." (Section 87400(b).) The 
permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in 
which a state administrative official participated while he or she served as a state administrative 
official. "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, 
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or 
state administrative agency . . .. " (Section 87400(c).) 
As a Staff Services Manager II, you were a state administrative official because you 
worked on the approval of licenses. Therefore, the permanent ban applies to you and your work 
as a Staff Services Manager II. You have not provided any information indicating that you were 
a state administrative official as a Staff Services Manager I. The permanent ban would not apply 
to your work as a Staff Services Manager I unless you engaged in any judicial, quasi-judicial or 
other proceeding as described above in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial 
capacity. The attached fact sheet contains more information about the permanent ban. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question: Can an employee of the Department of Alcohol and Drug Programs (DADP) who is interested in separating from State service to work for a private provider of medical, mental health, and substance abuse services licensed and certified by DADP lawfully take a position coordinating with government agencies for program expansion, attending meetings regarding government proposals and policies, and assisting in data collection, given the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: The one-year ban applies to your position as Staff Services Manager I because it was designated in your agency's conflict-of-interest code. You are prohibited for one year from conversing, corresponding, attending meetings, or otherwise communicating with your former agency for the purpose of influencing its governmental decisions. However, the proposed duties appear to be structured to avoid triggering the one-year ban because you would not be appearing before your former agency for the purpose of influencing decisions and your name would not be included on correspondence with your former agency. The permanent ban applies to your prior work as a Staff Services Manager II because you participated in license approvals, which constitutes a 'proceeding' under the Act. The permanent ban would prohibit you from engaging in any proceeding involving specific parties relating to license approvals, but the proposed position as described would not appear to violate this restriction.

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks whether the one-year and permanent post-governmental employment bans ('revolving door' provisions) under the Political Reform Act would restrict her proposed employment with a private healthcare services company that is licensed by her former state agency.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The one-year ban applies to the requestor's position, but the structure of her proposed duties appears designed to avoid violations. The permanent ban applies to her prior work on license approvals, but the proposed position would not appear to violate it.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Informal advice letter with clear Facts, Analysis, and Conclusion sections. The question and conclusion are synthesized from narrative form. Document provides detailed regulatory framework for both one-year and permanent bans.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Can an employee of the Department of Alcohol and Drug Programs (DADP) who is interested in separating from State service to work for a private provider of medical, mental health, and substance abuse services licensed and certified by DADP lawfully take a position coordinating with government agencies for program expansion, attending meetings regarding government proposals and policies, and assisting in data collection, given the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The one-year ban applies to your position as Staff Services Manager I because it was designated in your agency's conflict-of-interest code. You are prohibited for one year from conversing, corresponding, attending meetings, or otherwise communicating with your former agency for the purpose of influencing its governmental decisions. However, the proposed duties appear to be structured to avoid triggering the one-year ban because you would not be appearing before your former agency for the purpose of influencing decisions and your name would not be included on correspondence with your former agency. The permanent ban applies to your prior work as a Staff Services Manager II because you participated in license approvals, which constitutes a 'proceeding' under the Act. The permanent ban would prohibit you from engaging in any proceeding involving specific parties relating to license approvals, but the proposed position as described would not appear to violate this restriction.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides informal advice to a state employee regarding whether she can work for a private company licensed by her former agency under the Political Reform Act's revolving door provisions. The analysis confirms the one-year and permanent bans apply but concludes the proposed position as described would likely not violate either restriction.
